desc: Beirut is a city break that only makes sense if you build in uncertainty: Corniche walks and Raouche sunsets, one of the Levant's best food scenes, major archaeology at the National Museum, and layered neighbourhoods that still reward slow exploration when conditions are stable. For travellers who choose to go, the city offers real cultural depth and nightlife energy, but every trip needs flexible plans, close attention to official advice and realistic transfer timing.
hls: Fast access to Byblos or mountain escapes when conditions allow

todo.name: Beirut Corniche
todo.desc: Walk from Raouche toward the marina for sea air, people-watching and sunset light.

todo.name: National Museum of Beirut
todo.desc: Use the museum to frame Lebanon's deep history before taking day trips north or inland.

todo.name: Mar Mikhael and Gemmayzeh
todo.desc: Explore restaurants, bars and galleries while checking current opening patterns locally.

todo.name: Byblos day trip
todo.desc: Add the coast north of Beirut for harbour ruins, old souks and a different pace.

todo.name: Raouche and Pigeon Rocks
todo.desc: Time this for late afternoon or sunset, when the sea breeze softens the heat and the limestone stacks feel most dramatic.

todo.name: Hamra cafes and bookshops
todo.desc: Use Hamra for a lower-key afternoon of cafes, old Beirut institutions and people-watching away from the late-night bar strip.

wifi.note: Usable in many central hotels, cafes and coworking spots, but power cuts and neighbourhood-level disruptions mean mobile data and a battery pack are sensible backups.

prac.fltNote: Beirut uses one main airport close to the city, and London service is more realistically Heathrow-linked than LGW/LCY-linked. Route availability, timings and fares can change quickly with regional airspace and security conditions, so do not assume a schedule is stable until very near departure and re-check before heading to the airport.
prac.currency: Lebanese pound (LBP); US dollars are also widely used in some travel contexts
prac.lang: Arabic; French and English are common in Beirut and tourism services
prac.visa: British citizens can normally get a free single-entry tourist or family-visit visa on arrival at Beirut airport for one month, but rules can change quickly; passports with Israeli stamps can lead to refusal, so verify entry conditions before booking.
bestFor: Driver-supported coast or mountain day trips
Short urban stops with flexible planning
alerts: Monitor official travel advice closely; parts of Lebanon are under FCDO warnings and conditions can change at short notice.
Traffic can make the short airport run slow, especially if checkpoints, diversions or evening congestion build up.
Power cuts, card-machine failures and infrastructure disruption can affect hotels, restaurants and cash access.
Keep plans flexible and confirm restaurant openings, museum hours and intercity movements locally rather than assuming normal patterns.

sweet: May, June, September and October are the best balance for warm evenings and outdoor walking without the full July-August heat, but only if the wider security situation is stable enough to make a leisure trip sensible.
